title: Proverbs 6
subtitle: Four Warnings; Seven Abominations; The Cost of Adultery

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — Four Warnings: Surety, Laziness, and the Troublemaker

heb.paragraph: The opening warning targets the person who has ʿārab — put up security for a neighbour's debt. The verb means to intermingle or become entangled; the financial metaphor carries overtones of being trapped. The sage's advice is blunt: humble yourself and escape like a gazelle from a hunter.

The ʿāṣēl is Proverbs' recurring comic-tragic figure — the person who will not begin what needs doing. He is sent to observe the ant (nĕmālâ), who has no overseer yet stores provisions. The Hebrew root carries the sense of sluggishness and heaviness — inertia that collapses into poverty.

The ʾîš bĕliyyaʿal (worthless person) in vv. 12–15 is a deceiver who signals with winks and shuffling feet. Bĕliyyaʿal literally means 'without profit' or 'without rising' — a person of no moral value. In later usage it becomes virtually a proper name for extreme evil (cf. 2 Cor 6:15, Belial).

refs.note: The second ant passage — an extended narrative version of vv.9–11: the narrator walks past the sluggard's field and sees the thorns and weeds, drawing the same conclusion.
Paul's instruction: "the one who is unwilling to work shall not eat" echoes the Proverbs logic that diligence is connected to provision, and idleness to poverty.

notes.note: The surety warning is the first financial instruction in Proverbs. Pledging security for a stranger's debt was one of the most common paths to financial ruin in the ancient world. The advice is not uncharitable — it is realistic about human nature and the structure of social obligation. If you have done this, the instructions are urgent: go immediately, exhaust yourself in getting free, allow no rest until the obligation is resolved.

Wisdom from the ant. Proverbs regularly finds wisdom in creation — the ant, the badger, the lizard (30:24–28). This is not incidental. Wisdom is woven into creation; the observant person can read it from the world around them. The ant's wisdom is specifically: it acts without external compulsion (no commander) and it acts with long-range vision (stores food for winter during summer). The sluggard has both problems: he needs external pressure to act, and he cannot think beyond the present moment.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The ant passage is one of Proverbs' most sustained examples of observation-based wisdom. Alter notes the method: watch the ant, consider her ways, become wise. The ant has no overseer, commander, or ruler yet works diligently. The observation grounds the ethical instruction in nature rather than divine command — wisdom can be read directly from the observable world.

The seven-abominations list is a numerical proverb — a 'x, yes x+1' structure (six things the LORD hates, yes seven). Alter notes this is a standard ancient Near Eastern numerical device that creates suspense: you expect six, you get seven, and the seventh is the climax. The final item — 'one who stirs up conflict in a community' — is surprisingly social: the greatest abomination is not personal vice but community destruction.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The surety warning opens with a practical legal concern: going guarantor for a neighbour's debt. Waltke notes this is the book's least 'spiritual' instruction — but it is wisdom because financial over-commitment is a form of the pride that assumes one can guarantee what is not in one's control. The practical and the theological are never separate in Proverbs.

The seven abominations list is one of Proverbs' most memorable passages. Waltke notes the seven (a number of completeness) are structured from body-part to action: haughty eyes → lying tongue → hands that shed innocent blood → heart that devises evil → feet that run to evil → false witness → one who stirs up discord. The progression from inner attitude (eyes, heart) to outer action (hands, feet) encodes the Proverbs moral psychology: character is the source of conduct.

Calvin on the ant: 'The ant, a tiny creature without intelligence or authority, accomplishes more by industry than many humans accomplish with all their resources. God shows us in this small creature our own condemnation — we have a Creator, a Redeemer, a spirit, a conscience, and yet are outworked by an insect.'

Calvin on 'haughty eyes': 'The eyes are mentioned first because pride first betrays itself through the look. The proud man cannot conceal his contempt for others — it appears in his very gaze. This is the first of the abominations and the root of most of the others that follow.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'if you have put up security for your neighbour' (im aravta lere'akha) explains the ancient Near Eastern surety system: a guarantor pledged their own assets against another person's debt. If the debtor defaulted, the guarantor was legally liable. The instruction is to extricate oneself from such commitments — the risk is disproportionate to the benefit.

The NET note on the 'six things the LORD hates, seven that are detestable to him' discusses the X / X+1 numerical pattern (also Prov 30:15–33; Amos 1–2; Job 5:19). The pattern suggests completeness with a climax — the seventh item is the culminating point. Here the social sin (sowing discord among brothers) is the apex of the list of individual sins.

hist.context: Chapter 6 collects four distinct warnings: financial surety (vv.1–5), laziness (vv.6–11), the troublemaker (vv.12–15), and the seven abominations (vv.16–19), before returning to the adulteress (vv.20–35). The ant passage (vv.6–11) is one of Proverbs' most famous — wisdom observed in creation, not just received in instruction. The ant has no supervisor, yet acts with perfect diligence. The sluggard is not just lazy; he is refusing to read the lessons written into creation.

sections.header: Verses 16–35 — Seven Abominations; The Cost of Adultery

heb.paragraph: Tôʿēbāh (abomination, detestable thing) is one of the strongest words in the Hebrew vocabulary of moral disgust — used for idolatry, sexual perversion, and now these seven things. The list is structured as six + seven (a Hebrew numerical device for "a complete set"): it climaxes with "one who stirs up conflict" — the person whose characteristic work is destroying community.

The adulterer "has no sense" (ḥāsar lēb, literally "lacks heart/mind"). This is Proverbs' standard diagnosis of the fool: not primarily wickedness but profound stupidity about how reality works. He thinks he is gaining something; he is destroying himself.

refs.note: Jesus' list of evils from the heart — 'sexual immorality, theft, murder, adultery' — overlaps significantly with the seven abominations of 6:16–19, confirming the sage's diagnosis of human corruption.

The adultery warning in vv. 20–35 extends the Decalogue's prohibition. Proverbs does what the law alone cannot: it shows the practical consequences — financial ruin, social shame, and the husband's relentless fury.

notes.note: The seven abominations are united by their relationship to community: they destroy the social fabric — through pride, deception, violence, scheming, impetuosity, false witness, and strife. The list moves from individual character (haughty eyes) to community destruction (stirs up conflict). God's hatred is particularly strong for things that tear apart the covenant community.

"A man who commits adultery has no sense." The Hebrew is stark: ḥāsar lēb — he lacks a heart, lacks mind, lacks understanding. Proverbs doesn't primarily frame adultery as a crime (though it is); it frames it as supreme stupidity. The man who does it doesn't understand what he's doing to himself. This is consistent with the book's entire approach: the fool is fundamentally blind.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The three comparisons (fire in bosom → burnt clothes; walking on coals → burnt feet; sleeping with neighbour's wife → not going unpunished) are one of Proverbs' finest sequences of analogical argument. Alter notes the first two are perfectly obvious physical causation; the third exploits the logical parallel. The obviousness of consequences 1 and 2 makes consequence 3 feel equally inevitable — which is the rhetorical point.

The husband's jealousy (qin'ah, zeal/jealousy) 'will not accept any compensation; he will refuse the bribe, however great it is.' Alter notes this is a surprising ending: throughout the chapter economic comparisons have governed the argument (surety, theft, value of wisdom). Now the one calculus that cannot be economic is the wronged husband's honour. No price can compensate it. The economic frame of the chapter breaks down at exactly the point where honour does.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The adultery section of ch.6 (vv.20–35) is the most economically argued of the book's adultery warnings. Waltke notes the comparison structure: thieves steal from hunger (v.30) and are treated with more mercy than the adulterer; but the adulterer destroys a soul (v.32), and the wronged husband's jealousy is implacable (v.34–35). The calculation is precisely inverted from the adulteress's implied offer of easy pleasure.

'A man who commits adultery has no sense; whoever does so destroys himself.' The Hebrew for 'has no sense' (chasar-lev) is literally 'lacks heart/mind.' Waltke notes the characterisation of the adulterer as 'heartless' is deliberate: the 4:23 command to 'guard your heart' is answered here by the portrait of the man who has, in the deepest sense, failed to do so. He is heartless — lacking the wisdom-orientation that the heart represents.

Calvin on 'destroys himself': 'The adulterer is not merely sinning against another but against his own soul — he is ruining the very faculty by which he could have chosen otherwise. This is the peculiar nature of sexual sin: it corrupts the judge (the heart) along with the action.' Calvin anticipates Paul's observation in 1 Cor 6:18 that sexual sin is uniquely self-destructive.

Calvin on the husband's jealousy: 'The wrath of the injured husband is a just and implacable anger — and this natural law of honour, which no bribe can satisfy, is itself a witness to the gravity of the sin. Even pagan law recognises what the adulterer pretends not to know: that this violation cannot be bought off.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'the reproofs of discipline are the way of life' (vetokhachot musar derekh chayyim) explains the word musar (discipline, instruction, correction) as the pathway — not merely a means to life but the road itself. Life is found in the disciplined process of wisdom formation, not at the end of it as a reward.

The NET note on chasar-lev ('lacking heart/mind') explains this is a standard Hebrew idiom for stupidity or moral obtuseness (also Prov 7:7; 9:4, 16; 10:13; 11:12; 12:11). The note traces the idiom: having a heart = wisdom orientation; lacking a heart = the absence of that orientation. The adulterer is not evil so much as fundamentally witless.

hist.context: The seven abominations list (vv.16–19) is notable for what tops it — haughty eyes — and what ends it: "a person who stirs up conflict in the community." The list traces an inward-to-outward movement: eyes (attitude), tongue (words), hands (violence), heart (planning), feet (action), false witness (speech), community conflict (social destruction). The adulteress passage (vv.20–35) that follows uses fire imagery (vv.27–28) to make the point graphically: adultery is picking up fire in your lap and expecting not to be burned.

ppl.name: The ant
ppl.text: No commander but perfectly diligent. Represents the internalized wisdom of someone who acts rightly without external compulsion.

ppl.name: The sluggard
ppl.text: Refuses to observe creation's lessons. Defined by delay and self-deception about consequences.

ppl.name: The troublemaker
ppl.text: The villain who uses body language to plot — winks, signals, motions. His work is conflict; his end is sudden destruction.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"There are six things the LORD hates, seven that are detestable to him: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood, a heart that devises wicked schemes, feet that are quick to rush into evil, a false witness who pours out lies and a person who stirs up conflict in the community."NoteThe numerical device "six... seven" (×,×+1) is a common Hebrew literary form used in Amos 1–2, Job 5:19, and elsewhere. It does not mean there are exactly seven; it means "here is a representative, climactic list."

src.title: Numerical Sayings
src.quote: The "x, x+1" numerical pattern appears widely in ANE and OT wisdom literature. Amos uses it in his oracle series; Job uses it; Proverbs uses it here and in 30:15–31. The form signals a climactic list — the final item is the key one. Here the climax is "one who stirs up conflict," suggesting that social destruction is the ultimate expression of the vices listed.
src.note: The numerical form places this list within a well-established literary tradition.

src.title: Fire Imagery
src.quote: The fire imagery of vv.27–29 (scoop fire into lap, walk on hot coals) is one of Proverbs' most vivid rhetorical devices. It argues from obvious physical law to moral law: just as you cannot handle fire without being burned, you cannot commit adultery without being burned. The universe has a consistent structure.
src.note: The fire argument functions as a physical analogy for moral law.

rec.text: Jesus' instruction on lust: "anyone who looks at a woman lustfully has already committed adultery with her in his heart." This deepens v.25's "do not lust in your heart after her beauty" — Jesus moves the prohibition from action to intention, consistent with his development of OT ethics throughout the Sermon on the Mount.
Paul's "if a man will not work, he shall not eat" is the practical application of the ant/sluggard passage. The NT endorses Proverbs' connection of diligence to provision.

rows.label: The Ant: Wisdom from Nature — 6:6–11
rows.text: The ant passage is Proverbs' clearest example of its observational method: watch how the world works, extract the principle, apply it to human life. The ant has no overseer yet works; humans have a Creator, a conscience, and a community yet are often slothful. Wisdom observes the discrepancy and takes the lesser creature as teacher.

rows.label: The Six Things the LORD Hates
rows.text: Haughty eyes, a lying tongue, hands that shed innocent blood... one who stirs up conflict in a community. The numerical proverb reaches its climax in the seventh: sowing division is the ultimate social sin.

hebtext.note: Tôʿēbāh (abomination, detestable thing) is one of the strongest words in the Hebrew vocabulary of moral disgust — used for idolatry, sexual perversion, and now these seven things. The list is structured as six + seven (a Hebrew numerical device for "a complete set"): it climaxes with "one who stirs up conflict" — the person whose characteristic work is destroying community.

The adulterer "has no sense" (ḥāsar lēb, literally "lacks heart/mind"). This is Proverbs' standard diagnosis of the fool: not primarily wickedness but profound stupidity about how reality works. He thinks he is gaining something; he is destroying himself.

tx.title: 'When you walk, they will guide you'
tx.content: MT: behistallekhkha tancheh otkha (when you walk, she/they will guide you). The pronoun is ambiguous — 'she' (referring to wisdom/the father's commands) or 'they' (referring to the commandments). LXX: when you go about, it will lead you. Most English versions take 'they' = the commands of vv.20–21.
tx.note: The ambiguity between wisdom personified (she) and the commands (they) is unlikely to be accidental — in Proverbs, keeping the commands and following Lady Wisdom are the same act. The grammatical ambiguity encodes the theological identification: to follow the commandments is to follow wisdom herself.

debate.title: The Seven Abominations: A Moral Hierarchy or Illustrative List?

positions.name: Illustrative, not exhaustive or hierarchical
positions.argument: The seven items are representative examples of the character dispositions the LORD finds detestable — not an exhaustive moral catalogue or a ranked hierarchy. The numerical structure (six/seven) signals completeness by example rather than completeness by enumeration.

positions.name: The final item is the climax: communal sin is the greatest
positions.argument: The X/X+1 structure places the final item (stirring up discord among brothers) as the climactic abomination. The movement from individual sins (lying, murder, deceit) to the communal sin of divisiveness suggests that covenant community destruction is worse than any individual sin — a counter-intuitive priority that Proverbs articulates in several places.

scores.label: Practical Wisdom in Financial and Social Life
Divine Character as the Ground of Ethics
The Ant: Creation as Wisdom's Teacher

themes.note: Proverbs 6 is the book's most practically diverse chapter — surety, laziness, seven abominations, adultery — united by the underlying question: what does it look like to be truly foolish? The lazy man, the dishonest man, the community-destroyer, and the adulterer all share the 'chasar-lev' character: they lack the wisdom-orientation that the heart must be guarded for.
